Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: VERIFIED(step 2-7): Clicking red block (4x3, ~40px) rotates perimeter blocks clockwise by 1 position. Clicking green block or colored blocks does nothing. Green strip at col 0 shrinks 5px per rotation.
- Object taxonomy: Red block (40px, left edge)=rotation trigger. Light green strip (col 0)=progress bar. Light green block (40px, right edge)=fixed decoration. Colored 2x2/4x4 blocks rotate on perimeter. Yellow 2x2 markers=fixed indicators. Black 2x2 blocks=HUD.
- HUD map: Left green strip (col 0) shrinks 5px per rotation from 64px initial. Top gray area has black blocks. Charcoal background.
- Win pattern: Rotate until green progress bar depletes. Level completes when strip reaches 0.
- Level log: Level 1: Click red block repeatedly (8 rotations) until green strip at col 0 shrinks from 64px to 0. Score=1.
- Cross-level notes: Levels have different layouts but same rotation mechanic via red block trigger.
Current level:
- World model: Level 2 has different layout: 3 rows of colored blocks in a 41x41 charcoal area. Red blocks at (25,13) and (34,13) likely triggers. Green blocks at (25,47) and (34,47) likely decorations. Multiple colored 2x2 blocks arranged in rows. Yellow markers at specific positions. Need to determine rotation mechanic for this layout.
- Goal model: Likely same as Level 1: rotate until progress bar depletes. Need to verify rotation behavior on new layout.
- Recent findings: ['Level 1 completed by 8 rotations', 'Level 2 has 3-row layout with multiple red and green blocks', 'Green strip still at col 0 (64px) - resets per level']
- Open questions: ['Which red block is the trigger in Level 2?', 'What blocks rotate in Level 2?', 'Same rotation direction?']
- Plan: Test clicking red blocks to find trigger, then rotate until completion like Level 1
- Strategy log: ['Level 1: grouping/symmetry analysis abandoned - all rotations equal. Solved by rotating until strip depleted.']
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(32,54) green block: no effect (step 4)', 'MOUSE(20,13) colored block: no effect (step 5)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.

Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The click on the middle red block (25, 13) caused all 75 nodes to change! This is very different from Level 1 where only the perimeter blocks rotated. Looking at the changes:
- The green strip (id=0) changed from N (light green) to B (black) - but wait, that's the color changing, not just position
- The green strip pixels went from 64 to 63 (rows 1-63 instead of 0-63)
- All the colored blocks shifted positions
This seems like a different mechanic. Let me look more carefully at what changed:
- id=11 (R, red block at 16,19) -> became c (charcoal) at 11,11
- id=12 (N, green block at 16,38) -> became R (red) at 16,19
- id=13 (S, sky blue at 17,23) -> became N (light green) at 16,38
- etc.
It looks like all blocks are shifting in some pattern. The red block moved to where the green block was, the green block moved to where a sky blue block was, etc. This looks like a cyclic shift of all blocks.
But the key observation is: the green strip shrunk from 64 to 63 pixels (1 pixel per rotation instead of 5 in Level 1). The "about 38 target actions" suggests I need about 38 rotations.
Let me check: if the strip shrinks by 1 pixel per rotation and starts at 64, then 64 rotations would deplete it. But the target is about 38 actions. Hmm, maybe the strip doesn't need to reach 0, or maybe it shrinks at a different rate.
Wait, looking more carefully: the strip went from 64 pixels (rows 0-63) to 63 pixels (rows 1-63). So it shrunk by 1 pixel, losing the top row. If this continues, after 64 rotations it would be gone. But the target is about 38 actions.
Let me just keep rotating and see when the level completes. Since the mechanic seems to be "click red block to rotate", I'll batch several rotations together.
Actually, let me first understand the layout better. The blocks seem to be arranged in a specific pattern. Let me look at the colored blocks that are 2x2 (the gameplay objects) and see if there's a pattern.
From the segmentation, the 2x2 blocks inside the charcoal area (id=10) are:
- Row 1 (top): S, b, p, Y, p at positions (17,23), (17,26), (17,29), (17,32), (17,35)
- Row 2: p, b at (20,23), (20,35)
- Row 3: g, S at (23,23), (23,35)
- Row 4 (middle): w, b, b, w, S, p, c, g, c, S, g, g at (26,17), (26,20), (26,23), (26,26), (26,29), (26,32), (26,34), (26,35), (26,37), (26,38), (26,41), (26,44)
- etc.
This is getting complex. Let me just focus on the rotation mechanic and keep clicking the red block until the level completes.
Given the "about 38 target actions" hint, I should try to batch rotations efficiently. Let me do a batch of rotations and check the strip progress.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Level 2: Clicking red block rotates all blocks. Green strip shrinks 1px/rotation (vs 5px in L1).
# Target ~38 actions. Let me batch rotations and monitor strip.
# Current strip: 63px (after 1 rotation)

# Let me batch 10 rotations to speed things up
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 25, 'col': 13}] * 10)

# Check strip
seg = current_frame.segmentation
green_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 10]
for n in green_nodes:
    b = n['boundary']
    print(f"Green strip: pixels={n['pixels']}, rows {min(p[0] for p in b)}-{max(p[0] for p in b)}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": "VERIFIED: Red block rotates all blocks cyclically. Level 2: 1 rotation tested, strip shrank 1px/rot.", "cross_level_notes": "Same mechanic but different strip shrink rate per level"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "3-row layout with red trigger at (25,13). All blocks rotate cyclically. Strip shrinks 1px/rotation.", "goal_model": "Rotate until strip depletes (~38 rotations needed)", "recent_findings": ["Red block at (25,13) triggers rotation", "Strip shrinks 1px per rotation (63px after 1 rot)", "All blocks shift positions cyclically"], "open_questions": ["Exact number of rotations needed?"], "current_plan": "Batch rotate until level completes", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
